question_text: A letter is known to have come either from TATA NAGAR or from CALCUTTA. On the envelope, just two consecutive letter TA are visible. What is the probability that the letter came from TATA NAGAR.
question_html: A letter is known to have come either from TATA NAGAR or from CALCUTTA. On the envelope, just two consecutive letter TA are visible. What is the probability that the letter came from TATA NAGAR.
solution_text: Let E 1 be the event that letter is from TATA NAGAR and E 2 be the event that letter is from CALCUTTA. Also, let E 3 be the event on the letter, two consecutive letters TA are visible. {tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} And {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{8}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{7}{/tex} [Since, if letter is from TATA NAGAR, we see that the events of two consecutive letters visible are {TA, AT, TA, AN, NA, AG, GA, AR}. So, {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{8}{/tex} and if letter is from CALCUTTA, we see that the events of two consecutive letters to visible are {CA, AL, LC, CU, UT, TT, TA}. So, {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{7}{/tex} {tex}\therefore P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_3}}}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{8}}}{{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{8} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{7}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{8}}}{{\frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{{14}}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{8}}}{{\frac{{22}}{{8 \times 14}}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{8}}}{{\frac{{11}}{{56}}}} = \frac{7}{{11}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 be the event that letter is from TATA NAGAR and E2 be the event that letter is from CALCUTTA.
Also, let E3 be the event on the letter, two consecutive letters TA are visible.
{tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}P({E_2}) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
And {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{8}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{7}{/tex}
[Since, if letter is from TATA NAGAR, we see that the events of two consecutive letters visible are {TA, AT, TA, AN, NA, AG, GA, AR}. So, {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{8}{/tex} and if letter is from CALCUTTA, we see that the events of two consecutive letters to visible are {CA, AL, LC, CU, UT, TT, TA}. So, {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_3}}}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{8}}}{{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{8} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{7}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{8}}}{{\frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{{14}}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{8}}}{{\frac{{22}}{{8 \times 14}}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{8}}}{{\frac{{11}}{{56}}}} = \frac{7}{{11}}{/tex}

question_text: There are two bags, one of which contains 3 black and 4 white balls while the other contains 4 black and 3 white balls. A die is thrown. If it shows up 1 or 3, a ball is taken from the 1st bag; but it shows up any other number, a ball is chosen from the second bag. Find the probability of choosing a black ball.
question_html: There are two bags, one of which contains 3 black and 4 white balls while the other contains 4 black and 3 white balls. A die is thrown. If it shows up 1 or 3, a ball is taken from the 1st bag; but it shows up any other number, a ball is chosen from the second bag. Find the probability of choosing a black ball.
solution_text: Since Bag I = {3 black, 4 white balls}, Bag II = {4 black, 3 white balls} Let E 1 be the event that bag I is selected and E 2 be the event that bag II is selected. Let E 3 be the event that black ball is chosen. {tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P({E_2}) = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} And {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{4}{7}{/tex} {tex}\therefore P({E_3}) = P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{7} + \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{7} = \frac{{11}}{{21}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since  Bag I = {3 black, 4 white balls}, Bag II = {4 black, 3 white balls}
Let E1 be the event that bag I is selected and E2be the event that bag II is selected.
Let E3 be the event that black ball is chosen.
{tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P({E_2}) = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}

And {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{4}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P({E_3}) = P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{{{E_3}}}{{{E_2}}}} \right){/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{7} + \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{7} = \frac{{11}}{{21}}{/tex}

question_text: There are three urns containing 2 white and 3 black balls, 3 white and 2 black balls, and 4 white and 1 black balls, respectively. There is an equal probability of each urn being chosen. A ball is drawn at random from the chosen urn and it is found to be white. Find the probability that the ball drawn was from the second urn.
question_html: There are three urns containing 2 white and 3 black balls, 3 white and 2 black balls, and 4 white and 1 black balls, respectively. There is an equal probability of each urn being chosen. A ball is drawn at random from the chosen urn and it is found to be white. Find the probability that the ball drawn was from the second urn.
solution_text: Let U 1 = {2 white, 3 black balls} U 2 = {3 white, 2 black balls} And U 3 = {4 white, 1 black balls} {tex}\therefore P({ \cup _1}) = P({ \cup _2}) = P({ \cup _3}) = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} Let E 1 be the event that a ball is chosen from urn U 1 , E 2 be the event that a ball is chosen from urn U {tex}_2{/tex} and E 3 be the event that a ball is chosen from urn U 3 . Also, P(E 1 ) = P(E 2 ) = P(E 3 ) = 1/3 Now, let E be the event that white ball is drawn. {tex}\therefore P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{5},P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{3}{5},P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_3}}}} \right) = \frac{4}{5}{/tex} Now, {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_2}}}{E}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) + P({E_3}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_3}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{5}}}{{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{5} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{5} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{3}{{15}}}}{{\frac{2}{{15}} + \frac{3}{{15}} + \frac{4}{{15}}}} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let U1= {2 white, 3 black balls}
U2= {3 white, 2 black balls}
And U3= {4 white, 1 black balls}
{tex}\therefore P({ \cup _1}) = P({ \cup _2}) = P({ \cup _3}) = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Let E1 be the event that a ball is chosen from urn U1, E2 be the event that a ball is chosen from urn U{tex}_2{/tex} and E3 be the event that a ball is chosen from urn U3.
Also, P(E1) = P(E2) = P(E3) = 1/3
Now, let E be the event that white ball is drawn.
{tex}\therefore P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{5},P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{3}{5},P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_3}}}} \right) = \frac{4}{5}{/tex}
Now, {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_2}}}{E}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) + P({E_3}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_3}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{5}}}{{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{5} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{5} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{3}{{15}}}}{{\frac{2}{{15}} + \frac{3}{{15}} + \frac{4}{{15}}}} = \frac{3}{9} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_text: By examining the chest X ray, the probability that TB is detected when a person is actually suffering is 0.99. The probability of an healthy person diagnosed to have TB is 0.001. In a certain city, 1 in 1000 people suffers from TB. A person is selected at random and is diagnosed to have TB. What is the probability that he actually has TB?
question_html: By examining the chest X ray, the probability that TB is detected when a person is actually suffering is 0.99. The probability of an healthy person diagnosed to have TB is 0.001. In a certain city, 1 in 1000 people suffers from TB. A person is selected at random and is diagnosed to have TB. What is the probability that he actually has TB?
solution_text: Let E 1 = Event that person has TB E 2 = Event that person does not have TB E = Event that the person is diagnosed to have TB {tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{1}{{1000}} = 0.001,P({E_2}) = \frac{{999}}{{1000}} = 0.999{/tex} And {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = 0.99{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = 0.001{/tex} {tex}\therefore P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{E}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.001 \times 0.99}}{{0.001 \times 0.99 + 0.999 \times 0.001}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.000990}}{{0.000990 + 0.000999}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{990}}{{1989}} = \frac{{110}}{{221}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1= Event that person has TB
E2= Event that person does not have TB
E = Event that the person is diagnosed to have TB
{tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{1}{{1000}} = 0.001,P({E_2}) = \frac{{999}}{{1000}} = 0.999{/tex}
And {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = 0.99{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = 0.001{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{E}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.001 \times 0.99}}{{0.001 \times 0.99 + 0.999 \times 0.001}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.000990}}{{0.000990 + 0.000999}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{990}}{{1989}} = \frac{{110}}{{221}}{/tex}

solution_text: Let E 1 = Event that item is manufactured on A. E 2 = Event that an item is manufactured on B. E 3 = Event that an item is manufactured on C. E2 = Event that an item is manufactured on B.

Let E be the event that an item is defective.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P(E1) = {tex} \frac{{50}}{{100}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex} \frac{{30}}{{100}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} and P(E3) = {tex}\frac{{20}}{{100}} = \frac{1}{5}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{50}}{/tex}, {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{50}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_3}}}} \right) = \frac{3}{{100}}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{{50}}}}{{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{{50}} + \frac{3}{{10}} \cdot \frac{1}{{50}} + \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{{100}}}}{/tex}

question_text: A bag contains (2n + 1) coins. It is known that n of these coins have a head on both sides where as the rest of the coins are fair. A coin is picked up at random from the bag and is tossed. If the probability that the toss results in a head is {tex}\frac{{31}}{{42}}{/tex} , determine the value of n.
question_html: A bag contains (2n + 1) coins. It is known that n of these coins have a head on both sides where as the rest of the coins are fair. A coin is picked up at random from the bag and is tossed. If the probability that the toss results in a head is {tex}\frac{{31}}{{42}}{/tex}, determine the value of n.
solution_text: Given, n coins have head on both sides and (n+1) coins are fair coins. Let E 1 = Event that an unfair coin is selected. E 2 = Event that a fair coin is selected. E = Event that the toss results in a head. {tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{n}{{2n + 1}}{/tex} and {tex}P({E_2}) = \frac{{n + 1}}{{2n + 1}}{/tex} Also, {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = 1{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore P(E) = P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{n}{{2n + 1}} \cdot 1 + \frac{{n + 1}}{{2n + 1}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{31}}{{42}} = \frac{{2n + n + 1}}{{2(n + 1)}} \Rightarrow \frac{{31}}{{42}} = \frac{{3n + 1}}{{4n + 2}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 124n + 62 = 126n + 42 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2n = 20 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n = 10
solution_html:
Given, n coins have head on both sides and (n+1) coins are fair coins.
Let E1 = Event that an unfair coin is selected.
E2 = Event that a fair coin is selected.
E = Event that the toss results in a head.
{tex}\therefore P({E_1}) = \frac{n}{{2n + 1}}{/tex} and {tex}P({E_2}) = \frac{{n + 1}}{{2n + 1}}{/tex}
Also, {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = 1{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P(E) = P({E_1}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2}) \cdot P\left( {\frac{E}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{n}{{2n + 1}} \cdot 1 + \frac{{n + 1}}{{2n + 1}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{31}}{{42}} = \frac{{2n + n + 1}}{{2(n + 1)}} \Rightarrow \frac{{31}}{{42}} = \frac{{3n + 1}}{{4n + 2}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 124n + 62 = 126n + 42
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2n = 20 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} n = 10

question_text: Two cards are drawn successively without replacement from a well shuffled deck of cards. Find the mean and standard variation of the random variable X where X is the number of aces.
question_html: Two cards are drawn successively without replacement from a well shuffled deck of cards. Find the mean and standard variation of the random variable X where X is the number of aces.
solution_text: Let X denotes a random variable of number of aces. {tex}\therefore {/tex} X = 0, 1, 2 Now, {tex}P(X = 0) = \frac{{48}}{{52}} \cdot \frac{{47}}{{51}} = \frac{{2256}}{{2652}}{/tex} {tex}P(X = 1) = \frac{{48}}{{52}} \cdot \frac{4}{{51}} + \frac{4}{{52}} \cdot \frac{{48}}{{51}} = \frac{{384}}{{2652}}{/tex} {tex}P(X = 2) = \frac{4}{{52}} \cdot \frac{3}{{51}} = \frac{{12}}{{2652}}{/tex} X 0 1 2 P(X) {tex}\frac{{2256}}{{2652}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{384}}{{2652}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{12}}{{2652}}{/tex} XP(X) 0 {tex}\frac{{384}}{{2652}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{24}}{{2652}}{/tex} X 2 P(X) 0 {tex}\frac{{384}}{{2652}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{48}}{{2652}}{/tex} We know that, Mean {tex}\left( \mu \right) = E(X) = \sum {XP(X)} {/tex} {tex} = 0 + \frac{{384}}{{2652}} + \frac{{24}}{{2652}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{408}}{{2652}} = \frac{2}{{13}}{/tex} Also, Var(X) = E(X 2 ) - [E(X)] 2 {tex} = {\sum {{X^2}P(X) - \left[ {E(X)} \right]} ^2}{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {0 + \frac{{384}}{{2652}} + \frac{{48}}{{2652}}} \right] - {\left( {\frac{2}{{13}}} \right)^2}\left[ {\because E(X) = \frac{2}{{13}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{432}}{{2652}} - \frac{4}{{169}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.1628 - 0.0236 = 0.1391{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Standard deviation {tex} = \sqrt {Var(X)} = \sqrt {0.139} = 0.373{/tex} (approx)
solution_html:
Let X denotes a random variable of number of aces.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} X = 0, 1, 2
Now, {tex}P(X = 0) = \frac{{48}}{{52}} \cdot \frac{{47}}{{51}} = \frac{{2256}}{{2652}}{/tex}

{tex}P(X = 2) = \frac{4}{{52}} \cdot \frac{3}{{51}} = \frac{{12}}{{2652}}{/tex}

We know that, Mean {tex}\left( \mu \right) = E(X) = \sum {XP(X)} {/tex}

options.explanation: Here, P(A) = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{3}{10}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}P\left(A^{\prime} / B^{\prime}\right)=\frac{P\left(A^{\prime} \cap B^{\prime}\right)}{P(B')}=\frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{1-P(B)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-[P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B)]}{1-P(B)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-\left(\frac{2}{5}+\frac{3}{10}-\frac{1}{5}\right)}{1-\frac{3}{10}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-\left(\frac{4+3-2}{10}\right)}{\frac{7}{10}}=\frac{1-\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{7}{10}}=\frac{5}{7}{/tex} And {tex}P\left(B^{\prime} / A^{\prime}\right)=\frac{P\left(B^{\prime} \cap A^{\prime}\right)}{P\left(A^{\prime}\right)}=\frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{1-P(A)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-\frac{1}{2}}{1-\frac{2}{5}}=\frac{1 / 2}{3 / 5}=\frac{5}{6} \left[\because P(A \cup B)=\frac{1}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore P\left(A^{\prime} / B^{\prime}\right) \cdot P\left(B^{\prime} / A^{\prime}\right)=\frac{5}{7} \cdot \frac{5}{6}=\frac{25}{42}{/tex}

question_html: Let A and B be two events such that P(A) = {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}, P(B) = {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cup \mathrm{B})=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}. Then {tex}P(A | B) \cdot P\left(A^{\prime} / B\right){/tex}is equal to

options.explanation: Here, P(A) = {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\because {/tex} P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) {tex}\Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=\frac{3}{8}+\frac{5}{8}-\frac{3}{4}=\frac{3+5-6}{8}=\frac{2}{8}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\because P(A / B)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=\frac{1 / 4}{5 / 8}=\frac{8}{20}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} And {tex}P\left(A^{\prime} / B\right)=\frac{P\left(A^{\prime} \cap B\right)}{P(B)}=\frac{P(B)-P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{5}{8}-\frac{1}{4}}{\frac{5}{8}}=\frac{\frac{5-2}{8}}{\frac{5}{8}}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore P(\frac {A}{ B}) \cdot P\left(\frac {A^{\prime}}{ B}\right)=\frac{2}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{5}=\frac{6}{25}{/tex}

question_html: Two events E and F are independent. If P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 then P(E|F) - P(F|E) equals

question_html: Suppose a random variable X follows the binomial distribution with parameters n and p, where {tex}0<p<1{/tex}. If {tex}\frac{P(x=r) }{ P(x=n-r)}{/tex}is independent of n and r, then p equals

options.explanation: {tex}\because P(X=r)= ~^{n}C_r(p)^{r}(q)^{n-r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n !}{(n-r) ! r !}(p)^{r}(1-p)^{n-r}[\because q=1-p] \ldots(i){/tex} {tex}P(X=0)=(1-p)^{n}{/tex} And {tex}P(X=n-r)=^{n} C_{n-r}(p)^{n-r}(q)^{n-(n-r)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{n !}{(n-r) ! r !}(p)^{n-r}(1-p)^{-r}[\because q=1-p]\left[\because^{n} C_{r}=^{n} C_{n-r}\right] \ldots{/tex} (ii) Now, {tex}\frac{P(x=r)}{P(x=n-r)}=\frac{\frac{n !}{(n-r) ! r !} p^{r}(1-p)^{n-r}}{\frac{n !}{(n-r) ! r !} p^{n-r}(1-p)^{+r}}{/tex} [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)] {tex}=\left(\frac{1-p}{p}\right)^{n-\gamma} \times \frac{1}{\left(\frac{1-p}{p}\right)^{r}}{/tex} Above expression is independent of n and r, if {tex}\frac{1-p}{p}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{p}=2 \Rightarrow p=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

question_html: A and B are two students. Their chances of solving a problem correctly are {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, respectively. If the probability of their making a common error is, {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex} and they obtain the same answer, then the probability of their answer to be correct is

options.explanation: Let E 1 = Event that both A and B solve in the problem {tex}\therefore P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{12}{/tex} Let E 2 = Event that both A and B got incorrect solution of the problem {tex}\therefore P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let E = Event that they got same answer Here, {tex}P\left(\frac {E}{ E_{1}}\right)=1{/tex} , {tex}P\left(\frac {E}{E_{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{20}{/tex} {tex}\therefore P\left(\frac {E_{1}}{ E}\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{1} \cap E\right)}{P(E)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) \cdot P\left(E / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) \cdot P\left(E / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) \cdot P\left(E / E_{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{12} \times 1}{\frac{1}{12} \times 1+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{20}}=\frac{1 / 2}{\frac{10+3}{120}}=\frac{120}{12 \times 3}=\frac{10}{13}{/tex}

question_html: If A and B are independent, then P (exactly one of A, B occurs) = P(A) P(B') + P(B) P(A')
solution_text: True If A and B are independent events, we have, P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A)P(B) P(A’ {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A’)P(B) And P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B’) = P(A)P(B’) P(exactly one of A, B occurs) = P(A’ {tex}\cap{/tex} B) + P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B’) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(exactly one of A, B occurs) = P(A’)P(B)+ P(A)P(B’) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Statement is true.
solution_html:
True
If A and B are independent events, we have,
P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A)P(B)
P(A’ {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A’)P(B)
And P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B’) = P(A)P(B’)
P(exactly one of A, B occurs) = P(A’ {tex}\cap{/tex} B) + P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B’)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(exactly one of A, B occurs) = P(A’)P(B)+ P(A)P(B’)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Statement is true.

question_text: State if the statement is true or false: If A and B are two events such that P(A) > 0 and P(A) + P(B) > 1 then {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} / \mathrm{A}) \geq \frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{P(A)}{/tex}
question_html:
State if the statement is true or false:
If A and B are two events such that P(A) > 0 and P(A) + P(B) > 1 then {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B} / \mathrm{A}) \geq \frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{P(A)}{/tex}
solution_text: False {tex}\therefore P(B / A)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cup B)}{P(A)}>\frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{P(A)}{/tex}
solution_html:
False
{tex}\therefore P(B / A)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cup B)}{P(A)}>\frac{1-P(A \cup B)}{P(A)}{/tex}

question_text: If A, B and C are three independent events such that P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = p, then P (At least two of A, B, C occur) {tex}=3 P^{2}-2 P^{3}{/tex}
question_html: If A, B and C are three independent events such that P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = p, then P (At least two of A, B, C occur) {tex}=3 P^{2}-2 P^{3}{/tex}
solution_text: True P (at least two of A, B and C occur) {tex}=p \times p \times(1-p)+(1-p) \cdot p \cdot p+p(1-p) \cdot p+p \cdot p \cdot p{/tex} {tex}=p^{2}[1-p+1-p+1-p+p]{/tex} {tex}=p^{2}(3-3 p)+p^{3}{/tex} {tex}=3 p^{2}-3 p^{3}+p^{3}=3 p^{2}-2 p^{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
True
P (at least two of A, B and C occur)
{tex}=p \times p \times(1-p)+(1-p) \cdot p \cdot p+p(1-p) \cdot p+p \cdot p \cdot p{/tex}
{tex}=p^{2}[1-p+1-p+1-p+p]{/tex}
{tex}=p^{2}(3-3 p)+p^{3}{/tex}
{tex}=3 p^{2}-3 p^{3}+p^{3}=3 p^{2}-2 p^{3}{/tex}

question_text: If A and B are two events such that {tex}P(A / B)=p, P(A)=p, P(B)=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{5}{9}{/tex} then p = ________.
question_html: If A and B are two events such that {tex}P(A / B)=p, P(A)=p, P(B)=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}then p = ________.
solution_text: Here, {tex}P(A)=p, P(B)=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cup \mathrm{B})=\frac{5}{9}{/tex} {tex}\therefore P(A / B)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=p \Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=\frac{p}{3}{/tex} And {tex}P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5}{9}=p+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{p}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{5}{9}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{2 p}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5-3}{9}=\frac{2 p}{3} \Rightarrow p=\frac{2}{9} \times \frac{3}{2}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}P(A)=p, P(B)=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}and {tex}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cup \mathrm{B})=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P(A / B)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}=p \Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=\frac{p}{3}{/tex}
And {tex}P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5}{9}=p+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{p}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{5}{9}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{2 p}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5-3}{9}=\frac{2 p}{3} \Rightarrow p=\frac{2}{9} \times \frac{3}{2}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_text: If A and B are such that {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right) =\frac{2}{3} \text { and } P(A \cup B)=\frac{5}{9}{/tex} , then P(A’) + P(B’) = ________.
question_html: If A and B are such that {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right) =\frac{2}{3} \text { and } P(A \cup B)=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}, then P(A’) + P(B’) = ________.
solution_text: Here, {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{5}{9}{/tex} {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=1-P(A \cap B){/tex} [By Demorgan's Law {tex}\left(A'\cup B'\right) = \left(A\cap B\right)'{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3}=1-P(A \cap B){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=1-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\because P\left(A^{\prime}\right)+P\left(B^{\prime}\right)=1-P(A)+1-P(B){/tex} = 2 - [P(A) + P(B)] {tex}=2-[P(A \cup B)+P(A \cap B)]{/tex} {tex}=2-\left(\frac{5}{9}+\frac{1}{3}\right)=2-\left(\frac{5+3}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{18-8}{9}=\frac{10}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(A'\cup B'\right)=1-P(A \cap B){/tex} [By Demorgan's Law {tex}\left(A'\cup B'\right) = \left(A\cap B\right)'{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3}=1-P(A \cap B){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow P(A \cap B)=1-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\because P\left(A^{\prime}\right)+P\left(B^{\prime}\right)=1-P(A)+1-P(B){/tex}
= 2 - [P(A) + P(B)]
{tex}=2-[P(A \cup B)+P(A \cap B)]{/tex}
{tex}=2-\left(\frac{5}{9}+\frac{1}{3}\right)=2-\left(\frac{5+3}{9}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{18-8}{9}=\frac{10}{9}{/tex}

question_text: Let X be a random variable taking values x 1 , x 2 ,...., x n with probabilities p 1 , p 2 ,...., p n respectively. Then var(X) = ________.
question_html: Let X be a random variable taking values x1, x2,...., xn with probabilities p1, p2,...., pn respectively. Then var(X) = ________.
solution_text: {tex}\operatorname{Var}(X)=E\left(X^{2}\right)-[E(X)]^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\sum_ \limits{i=1}^{n} X^{2} P(X)-\left[\sum_ \limits{i=1}^{n} X P(X)\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\sum P_{i} x_{i}^{2}-\left(\sum P_{i} x_{i}\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\operatorname{Var}(X)=E\left(X^{2}\right)-[E(X)]^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\sum_ \limits{i=1}^{n} X^{2} P(X)-\left[\sum_ \limits{i=1}^{n} X P(X)\right]^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\sum P_{i} x_{i}^{2}-\left(\sum P_{i} x_{i}\right)^{2}{/tex}

question_text: A and B are two candidates seeking admission in a college. The probability that A is selected is 0.7 and the probability that exactly one of them is selected is 0.6. Find the probability that B is selected.
question_html: A and B are two candidates seeking admission in a college. The probability that A is selected is 0.7 and the probability that exactly one of them is selected is 0.6. Find the probability that B is selected.
solution_text: Let p be the probability that B gets selected. P(A is selected) = 0.7, P(A') = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.7 = 0.3 P (Exactly one of A, B is selected) = 0.6 P (A is selected, B is not selected;B is selected, A is not selected) = 0.6 {tex}P(A \cap B') + P(A' \cap B) {/tex} = 0.6 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} P(A) P(B') + P(A')P(B) = 0.6 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} P(A) P(B') + P(A') P(B) = 0.6 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (0.7) (1 - p) + (0.3)p = 0.6 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} p = 0.25 Thus, the probability that B gets selected is 0.25
solution_html:
Let p be the probability that B gets selected.
P(A is selected) = 0.7, P(A') = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.7 = 0.3
P (Exactly one of A, B is selected) = 0.6
P (A is selected, B is not selected;B is selected, A is not selected) = 0.6
{tex}P(A \cap B') + P(A' \cap B) {/tex} = 0.6
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} P(A) P(B') + P(A')P(B) = 0.6
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} P(A) P(B') + P(A') P(B) = 0.6
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (0.7) (1 - p) + (0.3)p = 0.6
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} p = 0.25
Thus, the probability that B gets selected is 0.25

question_text: The probability of simultaneous occurrence of at least one of two events A and B is p. If the probability that exactly one of A, B occurs is q, then prove that P(A') + P(B') = 2 - 2p + q.
question_html: The probability of simultaneous occurrence of at least one of two events A and B is p. If the probability that exactly one of A, B occurs is q, then prove that P(A') + P(B') = 2 - 2p + q.
solution_text: Since P (exactly one of A, B occurs) = q(given), we get {tex}P(A \cup B) - P(A \cap B) = q{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow p - P(A \cap B) = q{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow P(A \cap B) = p - q{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 - P(A' \cup B') = p - q{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow P(A' \cup B') = 1 - p + q{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow P(A') + P(B') - P(A' \cap B') = 1 - p + q{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow P(A') + P(B') = (1 - p + q) + P(A' \cap B'){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow (1 - p + q) + (1 - p(A \cup B)){/tex} = (1 - p + q) + (1 - p) = 2 - 2p + q.
solution_html:
Since P (exactly one of A, B occurs) = q(given), we get
{tex}P(A \cup B) - P(A \cap B) = q{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow p - P(A \cap B) = q{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow P(A \cap B) = p - q{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 1 - P(A' \cup B') = p - q{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow P(A' \cup B') = 1 - p + q{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow P(A') + P(B') - P(A' \cap B') = 1 - p + q{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow P(A') + P(B') = (1 - p + q) + P(A' \cap B'){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow (1 - p + q) + (1 - p(A \cup B)){/tex}
= (1 - p + q) + (1 - p)
= 2 - 2p + q.

question_text: 10% of the bulbs produced in a factory are of red colour and 2% are red and defective. If one bulb is picked up at random, determine the probability of its being defective if it is red.
question_html: 10% of the bulbs produced in a factory are of red colour and 2% are red and defective. If one bulb is picked up at random, determine the probability of its being defective if it is red.
solution_text: Let A and B be the events that the bulb is red and defective, respectively. {tex}P(A)=\frac{10}{100}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} , {tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{2}{100}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex} {tex}P(B | A)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}=\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{10}{1}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} Thus the probability of the picked-up bulb of its being defective, if it is red, is {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Let A and B be the events that the bulb is red and defective, respectively.
{tex}P(A)=\frac{10}{100}=\frac{1}{10}{/tex},
{tex}P(A \cap B)=\frac{2}{100}=\frac{1}{50}{/tex}
{tex}P(B | A)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}=\frac{1}{50} \times \frac{10}{1}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
Thus the probability of the picked-up bulb of its being defective, if it is red, is {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}.

Which is the required solution.

question_text: Two dice are thrown together. Let A be the event ‘getting 6 on the first die’ and B be the event getting 2 on the second die . Are the events A and B independent?
question_html: Two dice are thrown together. Let A be the event ‘getting 6 on the first die’ and B be the event getting 2 on the second die. Are the events A and B independent?
solution_text: A = {(6, 1), (6, 2), (6, 3), (6, 4), (6, 5), (6, 6)} B = {(1, 2), (2, 2), (3, 2), (4, 2), (5, 2), (6, 2)} {tex}A \cap B {/tex} = {(6, 2)} {tex}P(A) = \frac{6}{{36}} = \frac{1}{6},P(B) = \frac{1}{6},P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{{36}}{/tex} Events A and B will be independent if {tex}P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B){/tex} i.e., LHS = {tex}P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{{36}},{/tex} RHS = {tex}P(A)P(B) = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{{36}}{/tex} Here LHS = RHS Hence, A and B are independent events.
solution_html:
A = {(6, 1), (6, 2), (6, 3), (6, 4), (6, 5), (6, 6)}
B = {(1, 2), (2, 2), (3, 2), (4, 2), (5, 2), (6, 2)}
{tex}A \cap B {/tex} = {(6, 2)}
{tex}P(A) = \frac{6}{{36}} = \frac{1}{6},P(B) = \frac{1}{6},P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{{36}}{/tex}
Events A and B will be independent  if {tex}P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B){/tex}
i.e., LHS = {tex}P(A \cap B) = \frac{1}{{36}},{/tex} RHS = {tex}P(A)P(B) = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{{36}}{/tex}
Here LHS = RHS
Hence, A and B are independent events.

question_text: A committee of 4 students is selected at random from a group consisting 8 boys and 4 girls. Given that there is at least one girl in the committee, calculate the probability that there are exactly 2 girls in the committee.
question_html: A committee of 4 students is selected at random from a group consisting 8 boys and 4 girls. Given that there is at least one girl in the committee, calculate the probability that there are exactly 2 girls in the committee.
solution_text: Let A denote the event that at least one girl will be chosen, and B the event that exactly 2 girls will be chosen. We require P(B|A). Since A denotes the event that at least one girl will be chosen, A' denotes that no girl is chosen, i.e., 4 boys are chosen. Then P(A) = {tex} \frac{{^8{C_4}}}{{^{12}{C_4}}} = \frac{{70}}{{495}} = \frac{{14}}{{99}}{/tex} ... {tex}\left[ {^n{C_r} = \frac{{n!}}{{r!(n - r)!}}} \right]{/tex} Now {tex}P(A \cap B) {/tex} = P (2boys and 2 girls) {tex} = \frac{{^8{C_2}{.^4}{C_2}}}{{^{12}{C_4}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{6 \times 28}}{{495}} = \frac{{56}}{{165}}{/tex} Thus {tex}P(B|A) = \frac{{P(A \cap B)}}{{P(A)}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{{56}}{{165}} \times \frac{{99}}{{85}} = \frac{{168}}{{425}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A denote the event that at least one girl will be chosen, and B the event that exactly 2 girls will be chosen. We require P(B|A).
Since A denotes the event that at least one girl will be chosen, A' denotes that no girl is chosen, i.e., 4 boys are chosen. Then
P(A) = {tex} \frac{{^8{C_4}}}{{^{12}{C_4}}} = \frac{{70}}{{495}} = \frac{{14}}{{99}}{/tex} ...{tex}\left[ {^n{C_r} = \frac{{n!}}{{r!(n - r)!}}} \right]{/tex}
Now {tex}P(A \cap B) {/tex} = P (2boys and 2 girls) {tex} = \frac{{^8{C_2}{.^4}{C_2}}}{{^{12}{C_4}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{6 \times 28}}{{495}} = \frac{{56}}{{165}}{/tex}
Thus {tex}P(B|A) = \frac{{P(A \cap B)}}{{P(A)}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{{56}}{{165}} \times \frac{{99}}{{85}} = \frac{{168}}{{425}}{/tex}

question_text: Three machines E 1 , E 2 , E 3 in a certain factory produce 50%, 25% and 25%, respectively, of the total daily output of electric tubes. It is known that 4% of the tubes produced one each of machines E 1 and E 2 are defective, and that 5% of those produced on E 3 are defective. If one tube is picked up at random from a day’s production, calculate the probability that it is defective.
question_html: Three machines E1, E2, E3 in a certain factory produce 50%, 25% and 25%, respectively, of the total daily output of electric tubes. It is known that 4% of the tubes produced one each of machines E1 and E2 are defective, and that 5% of those produced on E3 are defective. If one tube is picked up at random from a day’s production, calculate the probability that it is defective.
solution_text: Let D be the event that the picked up tube is defective. Let A 1 , A 2 and A 3 be the events that the tube is produced on machines E 1 , E 2 and E 3 , respectively. P(D) = P(A 1 )P(D|A 1 ) + P(A 2 )P(D|A 2 ) + P(A 3 )P(D|A 3 ) P(A 1 ), {tex} \frac{{50}}{{100}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} , P(A 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} , P(A 3 ) {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Also {tex}P(D|{A_1}) = P(D|{A_2}) = \frac{4}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{25}}{/tex} {tex}P(D|{A_3}) = \frac{5}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex} Putting these values in (1), we get P(D) = {tex} \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{50}} + \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{1}{{80}} = \frac{{17}}{{400}} {/tex} = 0.0425
solution_html:
Let D be the event that the picked up tube is defective.
Let A1, A2 and A3 be the events that the tube is produced on machines E1, E2 and E3, respectively.
P(D) = P(A1)P(D|A1) + P(A2)P(D|A2) + P(A3)P(D|A3)
P(A1), {tex} \frac{{50}}{{100}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}, P(A2) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, P(A3){tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Also {tex}P(D|{A_1}) = P(D|{A_2}) = \frac{4}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{25}}{/tex}
{tex}P(D|{A_3}) = \frac{5}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex}
Putting these values in (1), we get
P(D) = {tex} \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{{50}} + \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{1}{{80}} = \frac{{17}}{{400}} {/tex} = 0.0425

question_text: Find the probability that in 10 throws of a fair die a score which is a multiple of 3 will be obtained in at least 8 of the throws.
question_html: Find the probability that in 10 throws of a fair die a score which is a multiple of 3 will be obtained in at least 8 of the throws.
solution_text: Here success is a score which is a multiple of 3 i.e., 3 or 6. Therefore, {tex}p(3 \text { or } 6)=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} The probability of r successes in 10 throws is given by {tex}P(r)=^{10} C_{r}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{r}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{10-r}{/tex} Now P (at least 8 successes) = P (8) + P (9) + P (10) {tex}=^{10} \mathrm{C}_{8}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{8}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}+^{10} \mathrm{C}_{9}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{9}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{10}+^{10} \mathrm{C}_{10}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{10}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{3^{10}}[45 \times 4+10 \times 2+1]=\frac{201}{3^{10}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here success is a score which is a multiple of 3 i.e., 3 or 6.
Therefore, {tex}p(3 \text { or } 6)=\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
The probability of r successes in 10 throws is given by
{tex}P(r)=^{10} C_{r}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{r}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{10-r}{/tex}
Now P (at least 8 successes) = P (8) + P (9) + P (10)
{tex}=^{10} \mathrm{C}_{8}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{8}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}+^{10} \mathrm{C}_{9}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{9}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{10}+^{10} \mathrm{C}_{10}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{10}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3^{10}}[45 \times 4+10 \times 2+1]=\frac{201}{3^{10}}{/tex}

X 1 2 3 4 5 6 7

question_text: Determine variance and standard deviation of the number of heads in three tosses of a coin.
question_html: Determine variance and standard deviation of the number of heads in three tosses of a coin.
solution_text: Let X denote the number of heads tossed. So, X can take the values 0, 1, 2, 3. When a coin is tossed three times, we get Sample space S = {HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT} P(X = 0)= P (no head) = P (TTT) {tex} = \frac{1}{8}{/tex} P(X = 1) = P (one head) = P (HTT, THT, TTH) {tex} = \frac{3}{8}{/tex} P(X = 2) = P (two heads) = P (HHT, HTH, THH) {tex} = \frac{3}{8}{/tex} P(X = 3) = P (three heads) = P (HHH) {tex} = \frac{1}{8}{/tex} Thus the probability distribution of X is: X 0 1 2 3 P(X) {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} Variance of X {tex}={\sigma}^2=\sum x_i^2p_i-\mu^2{/tex} where μ is the mean of X given by {tex}\mu = \sum {{x_i}{p_i}} = 0 \times \frac{1}{8} + 1 \times \frac{3}{8} + 2 \times \frac{3}{8} + 3 \times \frac{1}{8}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} (2) Now {tex}\sum {x_i^2{p_i}} = {0^2} \times \frac{1}{8} + {1^2} \times \frac{3}{8} + {2^2} \times \frac{3}{8} + {3^2} \times \frac{1}{8} = 3{/tex} (3) From (1), (2) and (3), we get {tex}{\sigma ^2} = 3 - {\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex} Standard deviation {tex} = \sqrt {{\sigma ^2}} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{4}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex} .

Let X denote the number of heads tossed. So, X can take the values 0, 1, 2, 3. When a coin is tossed three times, we get
Sample space S = {HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT}
P(X = 0)= P (no head) = P (TTT) {tex} = \frac{1}{8}{/tex}
P(X = 1) = P (one head) = P (HTT, THT, TTH) {tex} = \frac{3}{8}{/tex}
P(X = 2) = P (two heads) = P (HHT, HTH, THH) {tex} = \frac{3}{8}{/tex}
P(X = 3) = P (three heads) = P (HHH) {tex} = \frac{1}{8}{/tex}
Thus the probability distribution of X is:

X 0 1 2 3
P(X) {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}

Variance of X{tex}={\sigma}^2=\sum x_i^2p_i-\mu^2{/tex}

where μ is the mean of X given by
{tex}\mu = \sum {{x_i}{p_i}} = 0 \times \frac{1}{8} + 1 \times \frac{3}{8} + 2 \times \frac{3}{8} + 3 \times \frac{1}{8}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} (2)
Now {tex}\sum {x_i^2{p_i}} = {0^2} \times \frac{1}{8} + {1^2} \times \frac{3}{8} + {2^2} \times \frac{3}{8} + {3^2} \times \frac{1}{8} = 3{/tex} (3)
From (1), (2) and (3), we get
{tex}{\sigma ^2} = 3 - {\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
Standard deviation {tex} = \sqrt {{\sigma ^2}} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{4}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{/tex}.

question_text: Three machines E 1 , E 2 , E 3 in a certain factory produced 50%, 25% and 25%, respectively, of the total daily output of electric tubes. It is known that 4% of the tubes produced one each of machines E 1 and E 2 are defective, and that 5% of those produced on E 3 are defective. If one tube is picked up at random from a day’s production, calculate the probability that it is defective. Calculate the probability that the defective tube was produced on machine E 1 .
question_html:
Three machines E1, E2, E3 in a certain factory produced 50%, 25% and 25%, respectively, of the total daily output of electric tubes. It is known that 4% of the tubes produced one each of machines E1 and E2 are defective, and that 5% of those produced on E3 are defective.

  1. If one tube is picked up at random from a day’s production, calculate the probability that it is defective. 
  2. Calculate the probability that the defective tube was produced on machine E1.
solution_text: Let D be the event that the picked up tube is defective. Let A 1 , A 2 and A 3 be the events that the tube is produced on machines E 1 , E 2 and E 3 , respectively. Accoding to the Bayes Theorem , P(D) = P(A 1 )P(D|A 1 ) + P(A 2 )P(D|A 2 ) + P(A 3 )P(D|A 3 ) ...(i) {tex}P({A_1}) = \frac{{50}}{{100}} = \frac{1}{2},P({A_2}) = \frac{1}{4},P({A_3})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Also {tex}P(D|{A_1}) = P(D|{A_2}) = \frac{4}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{25}}{/tex} {tex}P(D|{A_3}) = \frac{5}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex} Putting these values in (1), we get {tex}P(D) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{50}} + \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{1}{{80}} = \frac{{17}}{{400}} = .0425{/tex} Now, we have to find P (A 1 / D). {tex}P\left(A_{1} / D\right)=\frac{P\left(A_{1} \cap D\right)}{P(D)}=\frac{P\left(A_{1}\right) P\left(D / A_{1}\right)}{P(D)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{25}}{\frac{17}{400}}=\frac{8}{17}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let D be the event that the picked up tube is defective.
Let A1, A2 and A3 be the events that the tube is produced on machines E1, E2 and E3, respectively.
Accoding to the Bayes Theorem , 
P(D) = P(A1)P(D|A1) + P(A2)P(D|A2) + P(A3)P(D|A3)  ...(i)
{tex}P({A_1}) = \frac{{50}}{{100}} = \frac{1}{2},P({A_2}) = \frac{1}{4},P({A_3})=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Also {tex}P(D|{A_1}) = P(D|{A_2}) = \frac{4}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{25}}{/tex}
{tex}P(D|{A_3}) = \frac{5}{{100}} = \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex}
Putting these values in (1), we get
{tex}P(D) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{25}} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{{20}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{{50}} + \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{1}{{80}} = \frac{{17}}{{400}} = .0425{/tex}
Now, we have to find P (A1 / D).
{tex}P\left(A_{1} / D\right)=\frac{P\left(A_{1} \cap D\right)}{P(D)}=\frac{P\left(A_{1}\right) P\left(D / A_{1}\right)}{P(D)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{25}}{\frac{17}{400}}=\frac{8}{17}{/tex}

question_text: A car manufacturing factory has two plants, X and Y. Plant X manufactures 70% of cars and plant Y manufactures 30%. 80% of the cars at plant X and 90% of the cars at plant Y are rated of standard quality. A car is chosen at random and is found to be of standard quality. What is the probability that it has come from plant X?
question_html: A car manufacturing factory has two plants, X and Y. Plant X manufactures 70% of cars and plant Y manufactures 30%. 80% of the cars at plant X and 90% of the cars at plant Y are rated of standard quality. A car is chosen at random and is found to be of standard quality. What is the probability that it has come from plant X?
solution_text: Let E be the event that the car is of standard quality. Let B 1 and B 2 be the events that the car is manufactured in plants X and Y, respectively. Now {tex}P({B_1}) = \frac{{70}}{{100}} = \frac{7}{{10}},P({B_2}) = \frac{{30}}{{100}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}P(E|{B_1}){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car is manufactured in plant X {tex} = \frac{{80}}{{100}} = \frac{8}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}P(E|{B_2}) = \frac{{90}}{{100}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}P({B_1}|E){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car has come from plant X {tex} = \frac{{P({B_1}) \times P(E|{B_1})}}{{P({B_1}).P(E|{B_1}) + P({B_2}).P(E|{B_2})}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}}}}{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}} + \frac{3}{{10}} \times \frac{9}{{10}}}} = \frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex} Hence the required probability is {tex}\frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let E be the event that the car is of standard quality. Let B1 and B2 be the events that the car is manufactured in plants X and Y, respectively. Now
{tex}P({B_1}) = \frac{{70}}{{100}} = \frac{7}{{10}},P({B_2}) = \frac{{30}}{{100}} = \frac{3}{{10}}{/tex}
{tex}P(E|{B_1}){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car is manufactured in plant X
{tex} = \frac{{80}}{{100}} = \frac{8}{{10}}{/tex}
{tex}P(E|{B_2}) = \frac{{90}}{{100}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex}
{tex}{/tex}
{tex}{/tex}

{tex}P({B_1}|E){/tex} = Probability that a standard quality car has come from plant X
{tex} = \frac{{P({B_1}) \times P(E|{B_1})}}{{P({B_1}).P(E|{B_1}) + P({B_2}).P(E|{B_2})}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}}}}{{\frac{7}{{10}} \times \frac{8}{{10}} + \frac{3}{{10}} \times \frac{9}{{10}}}} = \frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex}
Hence the required probability is {tex}\frac{{56}}{{83}}{/tex}.
